title: Hydrogen sulfide as a novel biomarker of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
abstract: Hydrogen sulfide (H<sub>2</sub>S) has recently been recognised as the third important gas-signalling molecule, besides nitric oxide and carbon monoxide. H<sub>2</sub>S has been reported to be produced by many cell types in mammalian tissues and organs throughout the actions of H<sub>2</sub>S-generating enzymes or redox reactions between the oxidation of glucose and element of sulfur. Although the pathological role of H<sub>2</sub>S has not yet been fully elucidated, accumulative data suggest that H<sub>2</sub>S may have biphasic effects. Briefly, it mainly has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant roles, although it can also have pro-inflammatory effects under certain conditions where rapid release of H<sub>2</sub>S in tissues occur, such as sepsis. To date, there have been several clinical studies published on H<sub>2</sub>S in respiratory disorders, including asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). According to previous studies, H<sub>2</sub>S is detectable in serum, sputum, and exhaled breath, although a gold standard method for detection has not yet been established. In asthma and COPD, H<sub>2</sub>S levels in serum and sputum can vary depending on the underlying conditions such as an acute exacerbation. Furthermore, sputum H<sub>2</sub>S in particular correlates with sputum neutrophils and the degree of airflow limitation, indicating that H<sub>2</sub>S has potential as a novel promising biomarker for neutrophilic airway inflammation for predicting current control state as well as future risks of asthma. In the future, concurrent measures of H<sub>2</sub>S with conventional inflammatory biomarkers (fractional exhaled nitric oxide, eosinophils etc) may provide more useful information regarding the identification of inflammatory phenotypes of asthma and COPD for personalised treatment.
publication: Allergology international : official journal of the Japanese Society of Allergology
